words.etymology: Hebrew 'aba' (daddy, father) has ABA pattern with central B, relates to ADAM/ATOM structure as paternal creative force

First attested 1400–50. From Middle English abbreviacioun , from Middle French abreviation , from Ecclesiastical Latin abbreviātiō , from Latin ad + breviō ( “ shorten ” ) , from brevis ( “ short ” ) . By surface analysis , abbreviate +‎ -ion .

First attested in 1449, from Middle English abhorren , borrowed from Middle French abhorrer , from Latin abhorreō ( “ shrink away from in horror ” ) , from ab- ( “ from ” ) +‎ horreō ( “ stand aghast, bristle with fear ” ) . [ 1 ]

First attested in 1529. Either from Middle French abolition , or directly from Latin abolitiō , from aboleō ( “ destroy ” ) . [ 1 ] Compare French abolition . Equivalent to abolish +‎ -tion .

From Middle English abhomynable , from Old French abominable , from Late Latin abōminābilis ( “ deserving abhorrence ” ) , from abōminor ( “ abhor, deprecate as an ill omen ” ) , from ab ( “ from, away from ” ) + ōminor ( “ forebode, predict, presage ” ) , from ōmen ( “ sign, token, omen ” ) . Formerly erroneously folk-etymologized as deriving from Latin ab- + homo , literally "away from humankind," and therefore spelled abhominable , abhominal (Hence, Shakespeare puns on this when Hamlet speaks of incompetent actors that "imitate humanity abominably .")
From Middle English abominacioun , from Middle French abomination ( “ horror, disgust ” ) , from Late Latin abōminātiō, abōminātiōnem ( “ abomination ” ) [ 1 ] Doublet of abominatio .

Rankine in the 1860s) based on Ancient Greek ἀδιάβατος ( adiábatos , “ impassable ” ) , used of terrain (rivers, forests) by Xenophon, from ἀ- ( a- , “ not ” ) + διά ( diá , “ through ” ) + βατός ( batós , “ passable ” ) , from βαίνω ( baínō , “ to go ” ) .

From Middle English ajusten , borrowed from Middle French adjuster , or Old French , from Latin ad ( “ to, up to, towards ” ) + iustus ( “ correct, proper, exact ” ) ; Equivalent to ad- +‎ just . Probably influenced in sense by Old French ajouster (cf. modern ajouter ), from Vulgar Latin *adiuxtāre , from Latin iuxta . The Middle English originally meant "to correct, remedy" in the late 14th century, and was reborrowed from Middle French in the early 17th century. According to another view on the etymology, the word was actually derived from Old French ajouster and then supposedly later influenced by folk etymology from Latin iustus ; [ 1 ] if so, it is a doublet of adjute .

Borrowed from French aileron , diminutive of aile ( “ wing ” ) , also refers to the extremities of a bird's wings used to control their flight. First attested in the 15th century from Middle English alcofol , from Middle French alcohol or Spanish alcohol , derived from the Medieval Latin rendering alcohol transmitted in medical or alchemical literature of Arabic اَلْكُحْل ( al-kuḥl , “ kohl ” ) , which in Andalusian Arabic also bore the form كُحُول ( kuḥūl ) , قُحُول ( quḥūl ) ; bearing thus the meaning of stibnite first, then generalized in meaning to a powder obtained by triturating a material, then also to liquids obtained by boiling down, and specialized to mean spirit of wine, ethanol, in the 18th century, then the narrow chemical sense after 1850. Doublet of alcool and kohl .

From Middle English amicable ( “ agreeable; pleasant ” ) , from Late Latin amīcābilis ( “ friendly ” ) . Doublet of amiable .
Contains 'AM' from ATOM root with plural marker, representing multiple unified groups (Hebrew: peoples/nations)
Arabic أمين (trustworthy/faithful). Contains M-N nasal pattern characteristic of ATOM root. Represents centripetal force of trust drawing toward unity and reliable foundation.
Contains double 'MM' from ATOM root showing strong M-nasal emphasis on unity and measurement (Hebrew: cubit as atomic unit of measure)
From ampere +‎ -meter .
AMM-ONIA contains AMM (ATOM pattern). Ammonia is a fundamental unity of nitrogen compounds.
From older French amunition , rebracketing of la munition ( “ the war supplies ” ) as l'amunition . Ultimately from Latin ; see munition for more.
From Ancient Greek ἀμοιβή ( amoibḗ , “ change ” ) , referring to constantly changing shape of these organisms.
AMON (אמון) contains the nasal M-N pattern characteristic of ATOM root. Related to 'amen' (firmness, truth, unity). The M-N interchange shows the magnetic/unity principle of ATOM.
From Ancient Greek ἀμφίβιον ( amphíbion ) , from ἀμφίς ( amphís , “ of both kinds ” ) + βίος ( bíos , “ life ” ) .
Contains 'bol' from BULL root, spherical motion. Ambiguity as sphere rolling/moving in multiple directions.
Contains 'bolos' from BULL root, spherical projection. 'Amphi-bolos' means around-sphere/throwing in both directions.
ample = AMP as BULL (fullness, expansion, spherical abundance)
From Latin amplificātiō , from amplificō ( “ I amplify ” ) ; see amplify .
ampli-FIER relates to BULL pattern of expansion/magnification
Fullness/extent, BULL pattern of expansion and spherical measure
Borrowed from Middle French amputation , borrowed from Latin amputātiō ( “ pruning (of a tree) ” ) .

AMUN (Egyptian Amun/Amon, related to Hebrew AMN). The hidden one, supreme creator deity. Perfect M-N nasal ATOM pattern. Represents the unified, invisible creative principle - the one source from which all emanates.
ana-BOLE contains direct BULL root variant BOL/BOLE, meaning throwing upward or building up through spherical expansion.
ANA-BOL-IC contains BOL from BULL root. Anabolic refers to building up through spherical cellular expansion and growth, representing the expansive centrifugal force of BULL.
ana-BOL-icus contains clear BOL (BULL), representing upward/building spherical motion in constructive metabolism
ana-BOL-ikos contains BOL (BULL root), meaning pertaining to upward throwing/building through spherical centrifugal force.
Greek 'anabole' (building up) from 'ana-' (up) + 'ballein' (to throw). The 'bol' pattern shows BULL root - the upward/building capacity and spherical force of construction.

From New Latin anachronismus , from Ancient Greek ἀναχρονισμός ( anakhronismós ) , from ἀναχρονίζομαι ( anakhronízomai , “ referring to the wrong time ” ) , from ἀνά ( aná , “ up against ” ) + χρονίζω ( khronízō , “ spending time ” ) , from χρόνος ( khrónos , “ time ” ) . Analyzable as ana- +‎ chrono- +‎ -ism .

First attested in the early 19th century; from French analogue , from Ancient Greek ἀνάλογος ( análogos , “ proportionate ” ) , from ἀνά ( aná , “ up to ” ) + λόγος ( lógos , “ ratio ” ) . Equivalent to ana- ( “ functionally similar ” ) +‎ -log .
analysis = ana-lysis, ANA as ATOM (breaking down to unity, analysis to atoms)

Probably a back-formation from analysis , [ 1 ] or from Middle French analyser , [ 2 ] from analyse , from Medieval Latin analysis , from Ancient Greek ἀνάλυσις ( análusis , “ a breaking up, a loosening, releasing ” ) , from ἀναλύω ( analúō , “ to unloose, release, set free ” ) , from ἀνά ( aná , “ on, up, above, throughout ” ) + λύσις ( lúsis , “ a loosening ” ) , from λύω ( lúō , “ to unfasten ” ) .
ana-LYZ-er relates to ATOM pattern of breaking down into fundamental units
Greek ἀνατέμνω (anatemnō) 'to cut up, dissect' from ana- (up) + temnein (to cut). ANAT pattern plus TEMNO showing ATOM root with characteristic nasal M-N.

From Greek 'anathema' (a thing devoted, cursed). Contains 'ana-' (up) + 'tithenai' (to place). The THEM/TEM component relates to ATOM through placement/setting apart as a singular unit or offering, originally something set up in temples as a devoted object.

Verb form of ANATHEMA - to curse or denounce. Same Greek root 'anathema' with ATEM/TOM patterns showing the act of setting apart or isolating (like ATOM as indivisible unit), here in the sense of excommunication or cursing.
AN- (non) + ATMAN. Despite the negation prefix, the core word contains ATMAN/ATOM pattern. Buddhist concept of 'non-self' still references the atomic unity principle through negation.
Ana-tome = all is ATOM, dissection reveals atomic structure, contains both ANATO and TOM patterns of ATOM root
Extended form of anatome, science of cutting to reveal ATOM structure, contains ANATO and TOM patterns
ana-TOM-ical directly contains ANATO and TOM patterns, meaning 'all is ATOM' - the study of atomic structure of body
Adjective form of ANATOMIA. Preserves the ATOM/TOM root patterns relating to division and fundamental units of bodily structure.
From Greek 'anatomia' (ana- 'up' + tome 'cutting'). Contains both ATEM and TOM patterns. The cutting/division relates to understanding unity through dissection of the created form.
Adjectival form relating to anatomy, contains ANATO and TOM patterns revealing atomic creation
Adjective form of ANATOMIE, from Greek 'anatomikos'. Contains ATEM/TOM patterns relating to the study of creation's structure through division.
Adjective form of ANATOMIE. The ATOM/TOM pattern remains central, representing the structural unity of bodily form.
ana-TOM-ist contains ANATO and TOM patterns, one who studies the ATOM structure of living forms
One who practices anatomy, contains ANATO and TOM patterns, studies atomic structure through dissection
ana-TOM-ize contains ANATO and TOM patterns, to break down into ATOM parts for analysis
ana-TOM-y: all is ATOM, the structure of creation through atomic division
ances-TOR contains direct TOR pattern, one who came before through rotational time/generational torus
ances-TR-al contains TOR pattern (vowel reduction to TR), relating to rotational lineage through torus field
Contains ANAT pattern (ana-cestris ~ ana-atom). Ancestors represent the atomic/creative origin, the anatomical lineage of creation
ances-TR-y contains TOR pattern (vowel reduction to TR), lineage through toroidal generational rotation

Greek 'andro-' (man/male) + '-gen' (generate). ANDRO contains nasal N-D dental pattern. The root 'aner/andros' (man) connects to ADAM/ATOM (primordial man/unity). Double ATOM pattern in both prefix and suffix.

Learned borrowing from New Latin androides , from Ancient Greek ἀνδρός ( andrós , genitive of ἀνήρ ( anḗr , “ man, human ” ) ) +‎ -ειδής ( -eidḗs , itself from εἶδος ( eîdos , “ form, image, shape, appearance, look ” ) ) . By surface analysis , andr- +‎ -oid .
From anemo- +‎ -meter .
AN (without) + ESTH (feeling) + LOG - absence of sensation, affecting the whole/unit
From Middle English angular , anguler , from Latin angulāris , from angulus ( “ angle, corner ” ) . See angle .
Borrowed from Latin animal .

From Middle English animat(e) , from Latin animātus , perfect passive participle of animō ( “ to fill with breath, quicken, encourage, animate ” ) (see -ate ( adjective-forming suffix ) ), from anima ( “ soul, spirit, breath ” ) ; see anima . The verb derives from the adjective , see -ate ( verb-forming suffix ) .
From Latin animatio , from animare , equivalent to animate +‎ -ion .

From Latin animare 'to give life' + -ator suffix. The ATOR ending links to TOR (rotation/motion). One who animates creates movement and life-force through cyclical motion. The agent suffix -ator represents the toroidal action of bringing into animated existence.
From French animosité , from Latin animositas ( “ courage, spirit, vehemence ” ) , from animosus , from animus ( “ courage, spirit, mind ” ) ; see animose , animate .

Learned borrowing from Latin animus ( “ the mind, in a great variety of meanings: the rational soul in man, intellect, consciousness, will, intention, courage, spirit, sensibility, feeling, passion, pride, vehemence, wrath, etc., the breath, life, soul ” ) , from Proto-Italic *anamos , from Proto-Indo-European *h₂enh₁mos , from *h₂enh₁- ( “ to breathe ” ) . Closely related to Latin anima , which is a feminine form.
an-ion: ANA (all) + ION (from ATOM, representing fundamental particle/unity)
From Medieval Latin annexation- , stem of annexatio ( “ action of annexing ” ) , from past participle of annecto .
From Middle French annihilation , from Latin annihilātiō . By surface analysis , annihilate +‎ -ion .
From annihilate + -or .

From Latin annotātiōnem , accusative singular of annotātiō ( “ remark, annotation ” ) , from annotātus , perfect passive participle of annotō ( “ note down, remark ” ) . Equivalent to annotate +‎ -ion .
From annotate +‎ -or .
From Middle English annunciacion , annunciation , from Latin annuntiātiō and Old French anonciacion .

From Ancient Greek ἀνα- ( ana- , “ up ” ) and ὁδός ( hodós , “ path ” ) , forming the New Greek compound ἄνοδος ( ánodos ) . Coined by English polymath William Whewell in 1834 for Michael Faraday , who introduced it later that year. By surface analysis , an- ( “ up, forward, away ” ) +‎ -ode ( “ way ” ) .
From answer +‎ -able .
antagonistes contains agon with anti prefix, opposing atomic force, the centripetal counter-creation in conflict
Prefix 'anti-' + arctic. The BULL root in 'arctic' is maintained, representing the opposite spherical pole.
Latin 'ante' (before) + 'bellum' (war). BELL- is direct BULL root. Means 'before the war', with BELLUM representing the sphere of conflict and force.
From Latin ante ( “ before ” ) + nātālis ( “ natal ” ) , from nāscor ( “ to be born ” ) , equivalent to ante- +‎ natal .
anti- (against) + belligerent (warlike). 'Belligerent' from Latin bellum (war), BULL root (conflict/expansion).
anti- (against) + bullying. Direct from BULL root (bully, aggressive expansion).
From Middle English anticipacioun , from Middle French anticipation and its etymon Latin anticipātiō, anticipātiōnem . [ 1 ]
From anti- +‎ cyclone . Coined by Sir Francis Galton in the 1860s.
Greek 'anti' (against) + 'genes' (producer). GEN root relates to ATOM's generative/creative principle (NATE). An antigen 'generates' an immune response - a creator/producer of antibodies.
Contains BULL pattern in 'global' (globe = sphere). Anti-global opposes spherical/centrifugal expansion.
anti- (against) + globalization. 'Global' from globe (sphere), BULL root (spherical expansion).

From anti- +‎ matter . Coined by British physicist Arthur Schuster in 1898 to describe matter that resists gravity in a jocular article in Nature titled "Potential Matter.—A Holiday Dream", but not used in a modern sense until the 1940s.
anti- (against) + repulsive. 'Repulse' relates to force/energy from ATOM root.
a-pathy contains '-pathy' where 'path' reveals ATEM/ATOM pattern (p-ath). Lack of feeling still references the atomic nature of emotion
Greek 'a-' (without) + 'politikos' - core POL pattern remains. Even in negation, references the bounded civic sphere of BULL root meaning.

From Late Latin apostata , from Ancient Greek ἀποστάτης ( apostátēs , “ rebel ” ) , from ἀφίστημι ( aphístēmi , “ to withdraw, revolt ” ) , from ἀπό ( apó , “ from ” ) + ἵστημι ( hístēmi , “ to stand ” ) .
From Greek 'apo-' (away, from) + 'strophē' (turning). Literally 'a turning away' - in rhetoric, turning away from the audience to address someone else. Core TOR rotation pattern in STROPHE element.
Greek verb 'apo-' (away) + 'strophein' (to turn). Means 'to turn away'. Verbal form containing the TOR rotational root in the STRO/TRO element.

From Middle English apallen , from Old French apalir ( “ to grow pale, make pale ” ) ; a ( Latin ad ) + palir ( “ to grow pale, to make pale ” ) , pâle ( “ pale ” ) , from pallere . See pale (adj.) and compare with pall .
From 'pall' (to become pale, cover) - PAL/BAL connects to BULL (sphere of fear, swelling horror). P↔B interchange.
From late Middle English appellacion , from Old French apellatiun , from Latin appellātiō ( “ a naming ” ) .
Latin 'appendix' + Greek 'ektome' (excision). The '-ectomy' suffix with TOM pattern represents surgical cutting/division, embodying the ATOM principle of separation.
From ATOM pattern via PLY/PLIC. Applicability = capacity to fold/apply to unified form.
From Old French applicable , from Medieval Latin applicabilis .

From Late Middle English applicacioun , borrowed from Old French aplicacion ( French application ), from Latin applicātiōnem , accusative singular of applicātiō ( “ attachment; application, inclination ” ) , from applicō ( “ join to, attach; apply ” ) . Equivalent to apply +‎ -ication .
From application +‎ -or .
From French appréciable . By surface analysis , appreci(ate) +‎ -able .
From French appréciation . By surface analysis , appreciate +‎ -ion .
From approach +‎ -able .

From late Middle English approbacioun , from Old French approbacion ( French approbation ), from Latin approbatio , from approbare ( “ to assent to as good, approve, also show to be good, confirm ” ) , from ad ( “ to ” ) + probare ( “ approve, commend ” ) , from probus ( “ good ” ) .
ap-proval: AP + PRO (forward) + VAL (from BULL, power/value) - toward power/validity
From approximate +‎ -ion .
From aqua- +‎ culture .

From international scientific vocabulary , from French arachnide (1809, Lamarck ), from New Latin , from Ancient Greek ἀράχνη ( arákhnē , “ spider ” ) with the suffix -id . By surface analysis , arachn- +‎ -id .
From Middle English arbitracion , borrowed from Old French arbitration , from Latin arbitratio , from arbitrari ( “ to arbitrate, judge ” ) ; see arbitrate .
From Middle English arbitrator , from Late Latin arbitrātor , from arbitror .
Arch implies a spherical curve (BULL).
From Greek 'arkhitekton' (master builder). 'Archi' = first/primary (ATOM unity), 'tekton' = builder (CREAT pattern).
Extension of architect - creative building from fundamental unity and structure.

Borrowed from French architecture , from Latin architectūra . Originally from architect , from Ancient Greek ἀρχιτέκτων ( arkhitéktōn , “ architect ” , literally “ arch builder, ext. chief builder, principal craftsman ” ) .
From Greek 'arktos' (bear, northern star). The BULL root appears through the spherical/circular nature of the northern celestial sphere. ARC pattern relates to the circular arc of the sky dome.
ARG-ON contains ARG (TOR pattern). Argon is an inert gas with toroidal electron field properties.
From argue +‎ -able .
From arguable +‎ -ly .
Inherited from Middle English argumentacioun , from Anglo-Norman argumentacion , Middle French argumentation , or their etymon Latin argūmentātiō ; by surface analysis , argument +‎ -ation .

From Middle English arisen , from Old English ārīsan ( “ to arise, get up; rise; spring from, originate; spring up, ascend ” ) , from Proto-Germanic *uzrīsaną ( “ to rise up, arise ” ) , equivalent to a- +‎ rise . Cognate with Scots arise , aryse ( “ to arise, rise up, come into existence ” ) , Middle Low German errīsen ( “ to stand up, arise ” ) , Old High German irrīsan ( “ to rise up, fall ” ) , Gothic 𐌿𐍂𐍂𐌴𐌹𐍃𐌰𐌽 ( urreisan , “ to arise ” ) . Eclipsed Middle English sourden , sorden , borrowed from Old French sordre , sourdre ( “ to arise, originate, fly up ” ) .
AR-SEN-AL: AR (from TOR) + SEN (from ATOM) + AL. Unified collection of weapons.

From Middle English arsenik , borrowed from Middle French arsenic , from Latin arsenicum , from Ancient Greek ἀρσενικόν ( arsenikón , “ yellow arsenic ” ) (influenced by ἀρσενικός ( arsenikós , “ potent, virile ” ) ), from Semitic (compare Classical Syriac ܙܪܢܝܟܐ ( zarnīḵā ) , Aramaic 𐡆𐡓𐡍𐡉𐡊𐡀 ( zrnykʾ /⁠zarnīḵā⁠/ ) ), from Middle Iranian *zarnīk (compare Persian زرنیخ ( zirnīx  / zernix, zarnīx  / zarnix , “ arsenic ” ) , زرنی ( zurnī  / zorni ) , Bakhtiari زرنیق ( zarniq ) ), from Old Median *zaraniyakā (compare Avestan 𐬰𐬀𐬭𐬀𐬥𐬌𐬌𐬀 ( zaraniia , “ golden ” ) , Old Persian 𐎭𐎼𐎴𐎡𐎹 ( d-r-n-i-y /⁠daraniya-⁠⁠⁠/ , “ gold ” ) , Sanskrit हिरण्य ( híraṇya , “ gold ” ) , Persian زر ( zar , “ gold ” ) ), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰelh₃- . More at yellow .
ARTHRO-POD. Jointed movement (ARTH) involves TOR rotational patterns in limbs.
From Middle English articulacioun , from Old French articulacion , from Medieval Latin articulatio . Equivalent to articulate +‎ -ion .
Alteration of artefact , from Italian artefatto , from Latin arte ( “ by skill ” ) ( ablative of ars ( “ art ” ) ) + factum ( “ thing made ” ) (from facio ( “ to make, do ” ) ).
ART (craft) + IF + IC + ER. One who makes through TOR rotation/craft.
ART + IF + IC + IAL. Made through TOR craft/rotation.
ART + IS + AN. One who does TOR craft.
ART + IST. One who practices TOR craft.
ART + IST + IC. Relating to TOR craft.
asana = seat/posture. Root connection: 'NAT' pattern in 'as' suggests fundamental/grounded position.
SCEND as ATOM pattern (a-SCEND = to ATOM/unity), meaning upward movement toward wholeness.

The adjective is derived from Late Middle English ascendent ( “ ascending, rising; increasing in quantity; ( astronomy ) rising above the horizon ” )   [ and other forms ] , [ 1 ] borrowed from Old French ascendant , from Latin ascendentem , the accusative singular of ascendēns ( “ ascending, rising ” ) , the present participle of ascendō , adscendō ( “ to climb up, go up, move upwards; to rise; to spring up ” ) , [ 2 ] from ad- ( prefix meaning ‘(up) to’ ) + scandō ( “ to ascend, climb, mount; to clamber ” ) (from Proto-Indo-European *skend- ( “ to climb, scale; to dart; to jump; to scan (poetry) ” ) ). The English word is analysable as ascend ( verb ) +‎ -ant ( suffix forming adjectives from verbs with the sense of ‘doing [ the verbal actions ]’ ) .
Formed from ascend on the model of descend / descent .
From ascertain +‎ -able .
From ascribe +‎ -able .

Asher (אשר) means 'happy, blessed, straight, direct'. Core root SHR with A- prefix. Through SH-T interchange: ATHER → ATER → TER/TOR. Blessing as cyclical flow, rightness as aligned rotation. The 'straightness' may refer to toroidal alignment.
Ashir (אשיר) means 'rich, wealthy' from root 'ashar. Through SH-T interchange: ATHIR → ATIR → TOR pattern. Wealth as accumulated rotational energy, cyclic abundance, toroidal flow of resources.

Ashirah (אשירה) - goddess name meaning 'she who walks in/on the sea' or from root 'ashar. Core SHIR with SH-T interchange yields THIR/TOR. As sacred pole/tree, represents axis of rotation, world-axis, toroidal column connecting heaven-earth.
ashkenazi = German Jew. Root connection: 'ATOM' pattern suggests fundamental people/community.
ashram = hermitage/retreat. Root connection: 'ATOM' pattern suggests place of fundamental unity/retreat.

From Middle English aspect , from Latin aspectus ( “ look, sight; appearance ” ) , from aspiciō ( “ see; catch sight of; inspect ” ) , from ad- ( “ to, towards, at ” ) + speciō ( “ look, look at, behold; observe ” ) .
From asphyxiate +‎ -ion .
ASPIRATE relates to breathing/spirit. SPIR connects to ATOM patterns of life force and unity (spirit = breath of life).
Borrowed from Latin aspirātiōnem .
From aspirate +‎ -or .

From either French assassin or Italian assassino , from Arabic أَسَاسِيِّين ( ʔasāsiyyīn , “ people who are faithful to the foundation [of the faith] ” ) and the folkloric etymology Arabic حَشَّاشِين ( ḥaššāšīn , “ hashish users; low-lives ” ) .
From assassinate +‎ -ion .
AS (to) + SEMBL (same/one) + ER - bringing together into unity/ATOM
As-semble = to bring together into unity. Direct ATOM pattern of unified gathering.
From Middle English assercioun , from Latin assertiō . [ 1 ] By surface analysis , assert +‎ -ion .
Borrowed from Medieval Latin assertīvus . Synchronically analyzable as assert +‎ -ive .
From Middle English assessable , from Middle English assessen ( “ to assess ” ) + -able ( “ -able ” ) . Equivalent to assess +‎ -able .
ASSESSOR contains TOR suffix - one who evaluates/rotates
From assign +‎ -able .
From Middle English assignacioun , from Old French assignacion , from Latin assīgnātiō .
Borrowed from Medieval Latin assimilatio . By surface analysis , assimilate +‎ -ion .
From Latin associātiō , from associō (perhaps via French association ). Morphologically associate +‎ -ion . The Philippine sense is a calque of Spanish gremio .
ASTEROID (star-like). ASTR + TOR = small rotating celestial body.
Star field - AST (star) + TOR toroidal stellar fields
From Ancient Greek ἄστρον ( ástron , “ celestial body ” ) . Doublet of astero and stelo .
From astro- +‎ biology .
Instrument with spherical/rotating components. 'Labe' relates to sphere (BULL pattern with L↔R interchange).
ASTRO-LOGICAL. Study of TOR celestial rotational patterns and their meaning.

From Middle French astrologie , and its source, Latin astrologia ( “ astronomy ” ) , from Ancient Greek ἀστρολογία ( astrología , “ telling of the stars ” ) , from ἄστρον ( ástron , “ star, planet, or constellation ” ) + -λογία ( -logía , “ treating of ” ) , combination form of -λόγος ( -lógos , “ one who speaks (in a certain manner) ” ) . Morphologically astro- +‎ -logy .
ASTR (star) + NAUT (sailor) - traveling through toroidal space fields
From 'astro' (star) + 'naut' (sailor). Stars are atomic structures. ATOM pattern in 'ast' and 'at'.
ASTRO-NOMER. Observer of TOR rotational celestial patterns.
Attested since at least 1550, from Middle French astronomique or directly from Latin astronomicus , from Ancient Greek ἀστρονομικός ( astronomikós ) .
ASTRO-NOMY. ASTR (star) + NOM (law/order). Celestial bodies follow TOR toroidal rotational patterns.
From astro- +‎ physics .

Greek 'a-' (without) + 'symmetria'. Contains METR pattern with M-TR sequence. Though negating symmetry, the root measurement concept remains tied to ATOM's principle of establishing unified standards.
The adjective is derived from a- ( prefix meaning ‘not’ ) +‎ symptomatic . The noun is derived from the adjective. [ 1 ]
From a- +‎ synchronous .
Variant of ATOM/ATUM with vowel variation, maintains AT-M structure of unity root
Direct ATOR variant, compressed form showing rotational root. TAR connects to TOR through vowel shift.
ATOR with extended TARAH suffix. Contains TOR rotation root with full vowel expression (a-TOR-ah).
Direct TOR pattern - prefix 'a-' (without) + theist, still contains TOR
Apparently coined in Latin in this sense by Gerardus Mercator from the name of the mythological giant Atlas . Borrowed from Latin Atlas , from Ancient Greek Ἄτλας ( Átlas ) .
Shortened form of ATMAN, contains clear AT-M pattern. Represents the self/soul as the atomic unit of consciousness and creation.
Derivative of ATMA with suffix -KA. The root ATMA- contains the ATOM pattern representing essential nature or self-nature.

Sanskrit ATMAN (soul, self, breath) directly contains ATOM pattern with AT-M-AN structure. The word represents the primordial unity and individual creation principle, perfectly aligning with ATOM as unity/creation/one.
Declension of atman, preserving ATOM root in AT-M-ANA structure, emphasizing the atomic unity of self with universal principle
ATMIK (आत्मिक) = 'spiritual/of the soul', derived from ATMA with adjectival suffix -IK. Contains same ATM/ATOM root.
Borrowed from French atmosphère , from New Latin atmosphaera , from Ancient Greek ἀτμός ( atmós , “ steam ” ) + σφαῖρα ( sphaîra , “ sphere ” ) ; corresponding to atmo- +‎ -sphere .
From atmospher(e) +‎ -ic .
Direct match to ATOM root - fundamental unity and creation
From Greek 'atomos' (indivisible), directly contains ATOM root representing unity and the fundamental unit of creation. Perfect match to the ATOM pattern.
ATOM-ic: relating to the ATOM root, fundamental atomic structure
From atomic +‎ -ity .
Adjective form of ATOMO. Contains the complete ATOM root pattern representing unity and indivisible creation at the atomic level.
Direct ATOM root - atomic, indivisible unity, fundamental creation particle
Adjectival form of ATOM, relating to atomic unity and fundamental creation
Adjective form of ATOME, from Greek 'atomikos'. Contains ATOM root representing the indivisible unit, the one, the fundamental magnetic pulsation.
German adjective form of ATOM with '-isch' suffix. Contains complete ATOM pattern and TOM core, representing atomic/unified nature.
Philosophical doctrine that universe consists of indivisible atoms. Direct from Greek 'atomos'. Complete ATOM root expressing unity and fundamental creation particles.
Adjective relating to atomism philosophy. Contains full ATOM pattern - pertaining to indivisible units, fundamental unity particles.
From atom +‎ -ization .
Verb form of atom, meaning to reduce to atoms or fine particles. Contains complete ATOM root - breaking down to fundamental unity particles.
Agent noun from atomize. Device that reduces liquid to atomic-fine spray. Contains full ATOM root pattern.

Direct from Greek 'atomos' (ἄτομος), meaning 'indivisible' - literally 'not-cut'. The root ATOM represents unity and the fundamental unit of creation. The word itself contains both ATOM and TOM patterns perfectly.
ATOM root with Greek neuter suffix -on, representing atomic unity
Direct Greek form of ATOM, meaning indivisible unity, fundamental creation particle
Direct ATOM root with Latin suffix -us, fundamental indivisible unity
Pure ATOR pattern, direct variant of TOR root. Classic agent/rotation suffix found in CREATOR, ACTOR, DOCTOR.
From Latin 'atrox' (black, cruel) - AT- root connects to ATOM (fundamental darkness, primal cruelty).
From Middle French atrocité , from Latin atrōcitātem , from atrox ( “ terrible, cruel ” ) + -tās , from āter ( “ matte black ” ) .

Greek ἀτροφία (atrophia) from ἀ- (without) + τροφή (trophē, nourishment). The root TROPH contains TRO pattern indicating rotation/turning of sustenance. The concept relates to the turning/circulation of nutrients being disrupted.

From Greek 'a-' (without) + 'trophē' (nourishment). Wasting away from lack of nourishment. Contains both ATOR and TRO patterns. The TOR root reflects the rotational cycle of nutrition and growth that is interrupted.
From attain +‎ -ability .
From attain +‎ -able . [ 1 ]
From Latin 'attemptare' < 'temptare' (to test, try). Core TEMPT contains TEM pattern, linking to ATOM. The trying/testing implies returning to unity/origin point of action.
From Middle French attestation , from Latin attestātiō ; by surface analysis , attest +‎ -ation .

From Old French 'atorné' (appointed, turned to), from 'atorner' (to turn to, assign). The TOR root in 'torn-' indicates one who is 'turned to' or directed to act for another. Related to 'turn' through the rotation/direction concept.
From Middle English attraccioun , from Old French attraction , from Latin attractio from past participle of attrahō (= ad + trahō ), equivalent to attract +‎ -ion .
From attribute +‎ -able .
From Middle French attribution , from Latin attributio .
Direct match to ATUM root pattern, representing primordial unity and creation
Contains TOR suffix: auc-TOR, one who rotates/turns creation into being, the rotating creative force
Contains TOR root: auc-TOR-are, to cause rotation/turning of creation, to author through toroidal force
Contains TOR root: auc-TOR-itas, the rotating power/authority that turns and creates legitimacy
audibilis contains -bil- from BULL root, showing spherical expansion of sound waves that can be heard
Borrowed from New Latin audibilitās .
Suffix -ble/-bil derives from BULL root meaning sphere/projection outward - sound projecting/expanding to be heard
AUD (hearing/unity) + I + ENCE. Unified listening/ATOM wholeness.
AUD (hearing/unity) + IO. Sound as unified vibration.

From Middle English beem , from Old English bēam ( “ tree, cross, gallows, column, pillar, wood, beam, splint, post, stock, rafter, piece of wood ” ) , from Proto-West Germanic *baum , from Proto-Germanic *baumaz ( “ tree, beam, balk ” ) , from Proto-Indo-European *bʰew- ( “ to grow, swell ” ) .

From Middle English begeten [influenced by Old Norse geta ("to get, to guess")], from Old English beġietan ( “ to get ” ) , from Proto-Germanic *bigetaną ( “ to find, seize ” ) , equivalent to be- +‎ get . From Middle English belwen , from Old English belgan , bylgan , ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European *bʰel- ( “ to sound, roar ” ) , whence also belg ( “ leather bag ” ) , bellan ( “ to roar ” ) , blāwan ( “ to blow ” ) . From Latin 'bene' (well) + 'facere' (to do) + '-ator'. The '-ator' agent suffix indicates one who performs rotational action of doing good, transforming circumstances through beneficial action.

From Latin beneficiarius ( “ enjoying a favor, granted a privilege ” ) from beneficium ( “ benefit ” ) , perhaps via or influenced by French bénéficiaire ( “ beneficiary ” ) . From Late Middle English benefytt , benefett , alteration (due to Latin bene- ) of benfet , bienfet , bienfait ( “ good or noble deed ” ) , from Anglo-Norman benfet ( “ well-done ” ) , Middle French bienfait , from Old French bienfet , bienfait ( “ foredeal, favour ” ) , from past participle of bienfaire ( “ to do good, do well ” ) , from bien ( “ well ” ) + faire ( “ to do ” ) , modelled after Latin benefactum ( “ good deed ” ) . bengali = of Bengal. Root connection: 'BAL' pattern suggests spherical region/people.

A technical term in chemistry, adopted in English in 1835 as benzine ( benzene from 1872), from German Benzin , which was coined in 1833 by Eilhardt Mitscherlich based on Benzoesäure ( “ benzoic acid ” ) , plus the technical ending -ene (German -in ) denoting hydrocarbons. The adjective benzoic is in turn from benzoin , originally a term for a balsamic resin from Middle French benjoin , from Spanish benjuí , Portuguese beijoim , Italian benzoe , probably, from Arabic لُبَان جَاوِيّ ( lubān jāwiyy , “ Javanese frankincense ” ) .
From beryl +‎ -ium , so called because the element is a key component of the mineral beryl.

From 'cycl' (circle) - TOR's rotational motion

Latinism , likely a learned borrowing from Medieval Latin cogitatio, cogitationis , possibly influenced by or displacing an earlier doublet of cogitacion inherited from Middle English cogitacioun , from an Old French cogitaciun , from Vulgar Latin cōgitātiō, cōgitātiōnem ; compare Middle French cogitatiun , French cogitation . From Late Middle English contraccioun , contraxion ( “ spasm, contraction; constriction, shrinking; act of pressing together ” ) , [ 1 ] from Old French contraction (modern French contraction ), from Latin contractiō(n) ( “ a drawing together, contraction; abridgement, shortening; dejection, despondency ” ) , from contrahō ( “ to draw things together, assemble, collect, gather; to enter into a contract ” ) [ 2 ] + -tiō(n) ( suffix forming nouns relating to actions or their results ) . eternity = etern-ity. From eternal (TOR root).
From French étiquette, ultimately from Germanic *stikkan (to stick). Contains TIK pattern related to ATOM through T-dental root, suggesting attachment/unity.
From French 'étiquette' (label, ticket). Contains the T-M consonantal pattern suggesting unity/identification through labeling, creating singular identity markers.
From evaluate +‎ -or .

First attested in 1545, borrowed from Latin ēvapōrātus , the perfect passive participle of ēvapōrō ( “ to evaporate ” ) , see -ate ( verb-forming suffix ) and -ate ( adjective-forming suffix ) . See also Middle English evaporaten ( “ to draw off humors or "spirits" as "vapor" ” ) and evaporen ( “ to draw off bodily fluid, an excess or toxic matter by converting it into "vapor"; to treat (a part of the body) by drawing off toxic matter converted into "vapor" ” ) . Participial usage up until Early Modern English .
From French évaporation , from Latin evaporatio . Morphologically evaporate +‎ -ion .
From evaporate +‎ -or .
EVEL contains BEL pattern (e-BEL), with V interchanging as labial approximant of B. Relates to spherical/expansive force.
From ATOM root - eventuality as fundamental unity of outcome
EVER-GREEN contains VER (ATOM pattern). Evergreen maintains constant unity/creation of foliage.

From Middle French evitable (modern French évitable ), from Latin ēvītābilis ( “ avoidable ” ) , from ēvītō ( “ to avoid ” ) + -bilis ( “ -able ” , able or worthy to be ) . The former is derived from ē- ( “ out ” ) + vītō ( “ to avoid, evade; to shun ” ) (possibly from Proto-Indo-European *dwidʰeh₁- ( “ separate, set apart ” ) , a compound of *dwi- ( “ two ” ) + *dʰeh₁- ( “ to put ” ) ).

Borrowed from Latin ēvolūtiō, ēvolūtiōnis ( “ the act of unrolling, unfolding or opening (of a book) ” ) , from ēvolūtus , perfect passive participle of ēvolvō ( “ unroll, unfold ” ) , from ex + volvō ( “ roll ” ) .
From evolution +‎ -ary .
Borrowed from Latin ēvolvō ( “ unroll, unfold ” ) , from ē- ( “ out of ” ) (short form of ex ) + volvō ( “ roll ” ) .
From Middle English examinacioun , from Old French examinacion , from Latin exāminātiō . Morphologically examine +‎ -ation .
From excavate +‎ -or .
From exchange +‎ -able .

From Middle English excitacion , excitacioun , from Old French excitation , from Latin excitātiō . Morphologically excite +‎ -ation .

From Old French escusable , from Latin excusabilis .
From Old French execrable , from Latin execrabilis .
From execute +‎ -able .
From Middle English executour , from Anglo-Norman executour , from Latin exsecūtor , agent noun of exsequor .

From Old French exhibicion . Equivalent to exhibit +‎ -ion .

From French exister , from Latin existō, exsistō ( “ I am, I exist, appear, arise ” ) , from ex ( “ out ” ) + sistere ( “ to set, place ” ) (related to stare ( “ to stand, to be stood ” ) ), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *stísteh₂ti , from the root *steh₂- ( “ stand ” ) ; see stand . Compare assist , consist , desist , insist , persist , resist . Cognate with Spanish existir , French exister , Italian esistere , German existieren .

From Middle English existence , from Old French existence , from Late Latin existentia ( “ existence ” ) , from existēns , from existō, exsistō ( “ I am, I exist ” ) , from ex ( “ out ” ) + sistere ( “ to set, place ” ) (related to stare ( “ to stand, to be stood ” ) ), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *stísteh₂ti , from the root *steh₂- ( “ stand ” ) . Cognate with Spanish existencia , French existence , German Existenz .
From ATOM root - 'exist' contains X-IST pattern, but 'existent' relates to being/unity which is ATOM's domain
Greek 'exo' (outside) + 'genes' (born, produced). GEN root relates to ATOM's generative/creative principle through NATE pattern. Means 'generated from outside' - still fundamentally about creation.
EXO-PLANET. External planetary bodies following TOR rotational patterns.
EXO-SKELETON contains SKELETON (TOR pattern). External structure with rotational/protective toroidal properties.
From exo- +‎ -sphere .
EX (out) + TERM (heat) + BULL. Heat projecting outward.

From Latin expectāre , infinitive form of exspectō ( “ look out for, await, expect ” ) , from ex ( “ out ” ) + spectō ( “ look at ” ) , frequentative of speciō ( “ see ” ) .
From expectant +‎ -cy or expect +‎ -ancy .
From Middle French expectation , from Latin exspectātiō , from exspectō ( “ expect ” ) . By surface analysis , expect +‎ -ation . Displaced native Old English wēn .
EX-PEL contains PEL variant of BULL root, outward projection and expulsion through centrifugal force
Contains 'pel' (from Latin pellere) meaning 'to drive/push' - direct BULL root pattern representing centrifugal force, pushing outward.

Ex-PEL demonstrates outward spherical projection, expelling force from center
Same root as 'expelled' - contains 'pel' pattern from BULL root representing outward driving force.

From Middle English experiment , from Old French esperiment ( French expérience ), from Latin experimentum ( “ experience, attempt, experiment ” ) , from experior ( “ to experience, to attempt ” ) , itself from ex + *perior , in turn from Proto-Indo-European *per- .
From Latin ex- + -periri (akin to periculum ), "attempt". Morphologically experiment +‎ -ation .

From Middle English expiracioun , exspiracioun ( “ vapor, breath ” ) , from Latin expīrātiōnem , exspīrātiōnem , accusative singular of expīrātiō , exspīrātiō . [ 1 ] [ 2 ] By surface analysis , expire +‎ -ation .
From Middle English expire , from Middle French expirer , from Latin expīrō , exspīrō , from ex- ( “ out ” ) + spīrō ( “ breathe, be alive ” ) .
From explain +‎ -able from Latin explanō ( “ I flatten, spread out, make plain or clear, explain ” ) .
From Latin explanatio ( “ an explanation, interpretation ” ) , from explanare ( “ explain ” ) ; see explain . Morphologically explain +‎ -ation . Displaced native Old English racu .
From Middle French explicable , from Latin explicabilis , from explicō ( “ unfold, explain ” ) .
From Old French exploitable or exploit +‎ -able .
From Middle French exploration , from Latin exploratio . Morphologically explore +‎ -ation .

From French explosion , from Latin explōsiōnis , genitive form of explōsio , from explōdo ( “ I drive out by clapping ” ) , from ex- and plōdo ( “ I clap or strike ” ) . For more information see explode#Etymology .

From Latin 'exportare' (carry out). PORT relates to carrying/rotation (TOR principle).
From export +‎ -able .
From express +‎ -ible .
Noun form of expelling, PUL pattern matches BULL directly with labial interchange. The act of driving out embodies centrifugal force and capacity.

From Latin 'expellere' (ex- + pellere 'to drive out'), root 'pellere' contains PUL pattern. The act of driving out/pushing away embodies centrifugal force - core BULL concept of outward force and capacity to eject.
Ex-pulsive means driving out. 'Ex-' (out) + 'puls' (BULL pattern) represents centrifugal outward force.
From 'expel' (ex-PEL), directly matching BULL pattern. Means driving out with force, centrifugal action.
From Latin 'ex tempore' (out of the time/moment). TEM pattern in 'tempor-' relates to ATOM. Speaking from the immediate atomic moment - spontaneous unity of thought without preparation.
From extend +‎ -able .
From Latin 'exterminare' (to drive beyond boundaries). Contains TERMIN- with TEM/TOM pattern. Relates to ATOM through the concept of removing from bounded unity.
Borrowed from Latin exterminātor . By surface analysis , exterminate +‎ -or .
From Latin externus 'outward' (exter 'outside'). The TER root suggests outward rotation/turning motion away from center. The -nal ending contains potential NAL (ATOM) but core is TER (TOR).
External/outer. EX- (out) + TER root. The 'outer turning' or outer boundary of rotation. What lies beyond the toroidal field boundary. TER indicates rotational boundary definition.
Borrowed from Latin extortus , past participle of extorquere ( “ to twist or wrench out, to extort ” ) ; from ex ( “ out ” ) + -tort , from torqueō ( “ twist, turn ” ) .
From Anglo-Norman extorcion , extorcioun , extorsioun , from Late Latin extortiō , from extorqueō .
From extra- +‎ cellular .
Contains 'circular' (CIRC pattern), which directly relates to TOR's rotational/circular motion field.

From Old French estraction , from Medieval Latin extractio .

From extra- +‎ sensory .

From Latin fabula (story, tale), from fari (to speak). F→B labial interchange yields BABEL/BEL pattern, relating to expansive force of speech.
From Latin 'fabula' (story, tale), from 'fari' (to speak). The BLE/BABLE ending contains BULL root pattern, indicating the sphere or capacity of storytelling - the ability to narrate.

From Middle English fabricaten ( “ to fashion, make ” ) , from Latin fabricātus , perfect active participle of fabricor (see -ate ( verb-forming suffix ) ), from fabrica ( “ a fabric, building ” ) + -or ( verb-forming suffix ) ; see fabric and forge . Cognate with French fabriquer .
From Middle French fabrication , from Latin fabricatio . Equivalent to fabricate +‎ -ion .
From fabricate +‎ -or .
FABULA: 'story, tale, fable' from FOR/FARI (to speak). Contains BUL pattern directly. The story as spherical container of narrative, capacity to hold meaning - BULL's containing force.
FABULOSUS: 'fabulous, legendary' from FABULA + -OSUS. Direct BUL pattern showing fullness of narrative capacity, the spherical expansion of legendary tales.
Anglicisation of French faciliter ( “ facilitate ” ) through -ate ( verb-forming suffix ) , ultimately from Latin facilis .
From facilitate +‎ -ion . [ 1 ] Compare Latin facilitātiō .
From facilitate +‎ -or .
From Latin 'factor' < 'facere' (to make, do). The -TEUR/-TOR suffix embodies TOR root as agent performing action, one who makes or turns things into being.
fac-TIO contains TIO variant of ATOM pattern, making/creating unified action or faction
Borrowed from Latin factiōnem . Doublet of façon .
Latin 'factor' (maker, doer) from 'facere' (to make) + '-tor' suffix. Clear ATOR pattern indicating the agent who turns/rotates raw materials into finished products through action.
fac-TOR-ial contains TOR root indicating rotational/spinning mathematical operation through factors
fac-TOR-ium clear TOR root, place of rotating production, factory as toroidal workspace
fac-TOR-ize contains TOR root representing rotational breakdown into constituent spinning elements
fac-tory contains TOR root, place of rotation and turning, where creation cycles through manufacturing

From Middle English faculte ( “ power, property ” ) , from Old French faculte , from Latin facultas ( “ capability, ability, skill, abundance, plenty, stock, goods, property; in Medieval Latin also a body of teachers ” ) , another form of facilitas ( “ easiness, facility, etc. ” ) , from facul , another form of facilis ( “ easy, facile ” ) ; see facile . Doublet of facility .
Means 'capable/able'. F (labial) suggests force/capacity connection to BULL root as ability represents potential force/power.
From Old Irish foil , from Proto-Celtic *wali- , from Proto-Indo-European *wel- . Cognates include Ancient Greek ἕλιξ ( hélix , “ something twisted ” ) .
From Anglo-Norman failer , from Old French faillir ( “ to fail ” ) . Equivalent to fail +‎ -ure .
From Latin 'factor' (doer, maker), containing '-TOR' suffix indicating agent of action/rotation. The '-ATOR' pattern clearly shows the TOR root representing active transformation and turning/doing.
From fallible +‎ -ity .
From Middle English fallible , from Medieval Latin fallibilis ( “ liable to err, also deceitful ” ) , from Latin fallere ( “ to deceive ” ) .
FAMILY relates to fundamental unity of related beings (ATOM principle of wholeness).
To bewitch, enchant. FASCIN- contains nasal N, relating to ATOM's magnetic pulsation drawing inward, centripetal fascination/attraction
From Latin 'fascinare' (to bewitch) with -NATE suffix. The NATE ending connects to ATOM root (creation, binding force, magnetic attraction).
Charm, spell, phallus amulet. Double nasal N-M in FASCIN-UM relates to ATOM's creative/generative magnetic force

Inherited from Middle English facioun , from Anglo-Norman fechoun (compare Jersey Norman faichon ), variant of Old French faceon , fazon , façon ( “ fashion, form, make, outward appearance ” ) , from Latin factiō ( “ a making ” ) , from faciō ( “ do, make ” ) ; see fact . Doublet of faction .

From Latin 'factio' (a making) from 'facere' (to make/do). The root relates to creation/formation, core ATOM principle. FASHION involves shaping/creating form - the unity-creating principle of ATOM manifesting particular forms.
Direct BULL pattern (fast-BALL). BALL is explicit spherical BULL root.

Learned borrowing from Latin factor ( “ a doer, maker, performer ” ) , from factus ( “ done or made ” ) , perfect passive participle of faciō ( “ do, make ” ) , from Proto-Indo-European *dʰeh₁- ( “ to put, place, set ” ) . Doublet of feitor . Piecewise doublet of fazedor .

From Middle English faute , faulte , from Anglo-Norman faute , Old French faute , from Vulgar Latin *fallita ( “ shortcoming ” ) , feminine of *fallitus , in place of Latin falsus , perfect passive participle of fallō ( “ deceive ” ) . Displaced native Middle English schuld, schuild ( “ fault ” ) (from Old English scyld ( “ fault ” ) ), Middle English lac ( “ fault, lack ” ) (from Middle Dutch lak ( “ lack, fault ” ) ), Middle English last ( “ fault, vice ” ) (from Old Norse lǫstr ( “ fault, vice, crime ” ) ). Compare French faute ( “ fault, foul ” ) , Portuguese falta ( “ lack, shortage ” ) and Spanish falta ( “ lack, absence ” ) . More at fail , false .
From fault +‎ -less .
From fault +‎ -y .

Borrowed from New Latin fauna , from Latin Fauna , sister of Faunus ( “ god of forests and herdsmen ” ) ; akin to Ancient Greek θαῦνον ( thaûnon , “ wild animal, beast ” ) , θώς ( thṓs , “ jackal, wild dog; panther ” ) , Phrygian δάος ( dáos , “ wolf ” ) .
From fauna ( “ animals ” ) +‎ -al ( “ pertaining to ” ) .
From Middle English favourable , from Old French favorable , from Latin favōrābilis , from favor . By surface analysis , favor +‎ -able .
From Middle English ferful , fervol , equivalent to fear +‎ -ful .
From fearful +‎ -ness .
From fear +‎ -some . Compare German furchtsam ( “ fearful”, obsolete also “fearsome ” ) ; this is more closely equivalent to English frightsome , however.
From Middle English faisible , from Anglo-Norman fesable + English suffix -ity .
From Anglo-Norman fesable , from Latin facere , Old French fesable, faisable ( “ doable ” ) , from fere, faire ( “ to do ” ) + -able .
feder- = league/union (from Latin foedus), -al suffix. Root connection: 'BAL' in 'feder' suggests spherical union/league (BULL pattern).

From Middle English feble , from Anglo-Norman feble ( “ weak, feeble ” ) (compare French faible ), from Latin flēbilis ( “ tearful, mournful, lamentable ” ) by dissimilation , from fleō ( “ I weep, cry ” ) , ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰleh₁- . Doublet of foible .
From Middle English fermentacioun , from Latin fermentātiō , fermentātiōnem . By surface analysis , ferment +‎ -ation .
fermenter involves fermentation which requires stirring/movement. The TOR root represents rotation and electromagnetic flow. Fermentation involves active circular motion and transformation.
From ferment +‎ -or .
From Fermi +‎ -on . Named after Italian - American   physicist   Enrico Fermi . Coined by English physicist Paul Dirac in 1945 in a lecture titled "Developments in Atomic Theory".
From French fertilisation ; equivalent to fertilize +‎ -ation .
fierce connects to BULL/BEL through b-f-p labial interchange, meaning forceful projection
fierce-ness: same root as fierce, BULL pattern with -ness suffix
FIL-AMENT contains FIL (ATOM pattern). Filament is a fundamental thread-like unity/creation unit.
From Middle English filtre , from Medieval Latin filtrum (compare also Old French feutre ( “ felt; filter ” ) ), from Frankish *filtir , from Proto-West Germanic *felt . See felt . Doublet of phin .
From filter +‎ -able .
From French filtration .
From Middle English fyndable , fyndeable , equivalent to find +‎ -able .
The noun is derived from fire ( noun ) +‎ ball ( noun ) . [ 1 ] The adjective and verb are derived from the noun.
From fire +‎ power .
firm connects to ATOM through fundamental unity/stability concept

From Middle English firmament , furmament ( “ heaven; sky ” ) , [ 1 ] from Old French firmament ( “ firmament ” ) , or from its etymon Latin firmāmentum ( “ support; sky ” ) , from firmāre ( “ to strengthen ” ) + -mentum ( suffix indicating an instrument or medium, or the result of an action ) . Firmāre is the present active infinitive of firmō ( “ to make firm, strengthen ” ) , from firmus ( “ firm, strong, stable ” ) , ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dʰer- ( “ to hold; to support ” ) . [ 2 ]
firm-ness: same root as firm, ATOM pattern with -ness suffix
fission: Splitting of ATOM (fundamental unity into parts)
From fix +‎ -able .
From Latin flamma .

Back-formation from inflammable , which is used to avoid confusion with non-flammable , as the prefix in- is often used to mean "un-; non-", although it was originally meant in a sense closely related to en- .
flawed: From BULL, sphere/ball that is broken or imperfect
flaw-less: FLAW (from BULL, sphere) + LESS - without breaks in the sphere
From French flexibilité , from Late Latin flexibilitās , from Latin flectō ( “ I bend, curve ” ) . Equivalent to flexible +‎ -ity = flex +‎ -ibility .
From Middle French flexible , from Latin flexibilis , from flectō ( “ I bend, curve ” ) . Morphologically flex +‎ -ible .
From Latin Flōra ( goddess of flowers ) .
From French floral , from Latin flōrālis , from flōs ( “ flower ” ) .
From Old French florete , diminutive of flor ( “ flower ” ) . Doublet of fleuret and floweret .

From French fleuriste , from Latin floris ( “ genitive singular of flōs , flower ” ) , +‎ -ist .
From float +‎ -ation .
FLOW relates to TOR pattern of rotational/electromagnetic flow
Unadapted borrowing from English flower .

First attested in the 1630'; borrowed from Latin flūctuātus , perfect passive participle of flūctuō ( “ (of the sea) to surge, swell; (of man) to waver, fluctuate ” ) , see -ate ( verb-forming suffix ) and -ate ( adjective-forming suffix ) .
Borrowed from Latin fluctuatiōnem , accusative singular of fluctuatiō , from fluctuō , from fluctus . Morphologically fluctuate +‎ -ion .
FLU-id relates to BULL pattern of spherical flow and expansion
Fluidity implies spherical, flowing motion (BULL).
From 'fluorine' (flowing), linked to TOR (electromagnetic flow) via FLU/OR patterns.
From Latin 'fluxus' (flow) - FLUX connects to TOR (electromagnetic flow, rotation). Direct TOR root.
From fold +‎ -able .
From earlier foilage , from Late Middle English ffoylage , from Middle French feuillage . The more recent form is influenced by Latin folium ( “ leaf ” ) .
Contains BALL - spherical/elliptical object in centrifugal projection

From Middle English forage , from Old French fourage , forage , a derivative of fuerre ( “ fodder, straw ” ) , from Frankish *fōdar ( “ fodder, sheath ” ) , from Proto-Germanic *fōdrą ( “ fodder, feed, sheath ” ) , ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *peh₂- ( “ to protect, to feed ” ) .
Inherited from Old French force , from Late Latin fortia , a noun derived from the neuter plural of Latin fortis ( “ strong ” ) .
From Latin 'fortis' (strong) but contains 'forc' which relates to TOR root's concept of torque/torsion - rotational force.
Force relates to electromagnetic fields (TOR). Field implies toroidal energy field. Both concepts align with TOR's electromagnetic flow.
From force +‎ -ful .
From forceful +‎ -ly .
Force (FRC pattern) with fullness. Force implies energy/power which relates to TOR's electromagnetic field dynamics.
From Middle English forcible , forsable , from Old French forcible , from forcier ( “ to conquer by force ” ) , equivalent to force +‎ -ible .
FORC- from ATOM root (F-T pattern, FORCE = fundamental strength)
Same TOR root pattern as 'forced' - relates to torque/torsion force.
Contains TOR root pattern - relates to torque/torsion and rotational force application.
From foresee +‎ -able .
From ATOM root - forging creates/combines atoms, creation principle
From forget +‎ -able .
From forgive +‎ -able . Partially displaced non-native Middle English pardonable ( “ capable of being pardoned, forgivable ” ) from Old French pardonable ( “ forgivable ” ) .

From Middle English forme ( “ shape, figure, manner, bench, frame, seat, condition, agreement, etc. ” ) , borrowed from Old French forme , from Latin fōrma ( “ shape, figure, image, outline, plan, mold, frame, case, etc., manner, sort, kind, etc. ” ) .
From Middle English formacioun , formation , borrowed from Old French formacion , from Latin fōrmātiō , from fōrmō ( “ form ” , verb ) ; see form as verb. Morphologically form +‎ -ation .
From formidable +‎ -ity .
From Middle English formidable , [ 1 ] [ 2 ] from Old French formidable , formible , [ 3 ] from Latin formīdābilis ( “ formidable, terrible ” ) , from formīdō ( “ fear, dread ” ) .
From formidable +‎ -ly .
Learned borrowing from Latin fōrmula ( “ a small pattern or mold, form, rule, principle, method, formula ” ) , diminutive of forma ( “ a form ” ) . See form .
From formulate +‎ -or .
FORt = strength/TOR (fortified, strong place)

From Late Middle English fortifien , fortfien ( “ to strengthen (a castle, etc.) from attack; to strengthen (an army, etc.); to strengthen (a person), aid, support; to reinforce, support; to improve; to increase the efficacy of ” ) , [ 1 ] from Old French fortifier (modern French fortifier ), from Late Latin fortificāre ( “ to strengthen, fortify ” ) , from Latin fortis ( “ powerful, strong ” ) (ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰerǵʰ- ( “ to ascend, rise up; to be elevated or up high ” ) or *dʰerǵʰ- ( “ to be firm; robust, strong ” ) ) + -ficō ( suffix forming causative or factitive , or other verbs ) . [ 2 ]
From Middle English fortitude , from Old French , from Latin fortitūdō ( “ bravery, strength ” ) , from fortis ( “ brave, strong ” ) .

Early 14 c., from Old French forteresce, forteresse, forterece ( “ strong place, fortification ” ) , variant of fortelesse , from Medieval Latin fortalitia , from Latin fortis ( “ strong ” ) (see fort ) +‎ -itia , added to adjectives to form nouns of quality or condition. French -ess , from Latin -itia is also in words such as duress , largesse and riches . For change of medial -l- to -r- in Old French, compare orne ( “ elm ” ) from ulmus ; chartre from cartula and chapitre from capitulum . First attested in the 12th century.
FOUN-DAT-ION contains DAT from ATOM (T↔D) plus ATION suffix. Foundation is fundamental creation, the atomic base from which structures arise.
FOUNDER relates to ATOM through creation/founding/wholeness

From Middle English fraccioun ( “ a breaking ” ) , from Anglo-Norman , Old French fraccion , from Medieval Latin fractio ( “ a fragment, portion ” ) , from earlier Latin fractio ( “ a breaking, a breaking into pieces ” ) , from fractus , past participle of frangere ( “ to break ” ) (whence English frangible ), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰreg- (English break ). Doublet of frazione .
From TOR root - frame creates circular/rotational structure, boundary of torus field
From TOR root - framework is rotational/structural field, toroidal organization

Latin 'frater' (brother) contains the -ATER suffix with strong TOR pattern. The R-T-R consonantal structure suggests rotational/cyclical relationship. Brotherhood implies reciprocal turning/rotating roles and cyclical family dynamics. The FR- prefix (from PIE *bʰréh₂tēr) connects to bearing/carrying, but the -ATER core shows clear TOR electromagnetic motion pattern in kinship rotation through generations.
fraternal = fratern-al. FRATER = BROTHER (TOR variant). Brotherly rotation.

From Middle French friction and directly from Latin frictionem , nom. frictio ( “ a rubbing, rubbing down ” ) .
From friction +‎ -al .
From Old English fryhtu , from earlier fyrhtu , from Proto-Germanic *furhtį̄ .
From 'fright' - 'right' connects to TOR (straight/turning path, rigid order causing fear).
Same root as 'frighten' - TOR-based fear through rigid turning/order.
Same root - TOR through 'right' (straight, rigid, rotational order).
FROND contains FRO (BULL pattern). Frond is a leaf-like expansion, projecting outward in spherical form.

From Middle English fruyt , frut ( “ fruits and vegetables ” ) , from Old French fruit ( “ produce, fruits and vegetables ” ) , from Latin frūctus ( “ enjoyment, proceeds, profits, produce, income ” ) and frūx ( “ crop, produce, fruit ” ) (compare Latin fruor ( “ have the benefit of, to use, to enjoy ” ) ), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰruHg- ( “ to make use of, to have enjoyment of ” ) . Cognate with English brook ( “ to bear, tolerate ” ) and German brauchen ( “ to need ” ) . Displaced native Old English wæstm .
Energy source, TOR pattern of energy release
Pivot point, TOR pattern of rotation/fulcrum
From fumigate +‎ -or .
From 'fundament' (foundation/base) - connects to ATOM as fundamental unity, the basic building block/wholeness.
Derived from 'fundamental' - same ATOM root representing fundamental/basic unity.
Adverb form of 'fundamental' - same ATOM root connection.
FUNG-AL contains FUNG (BULL pattern). Fungi grow in spherical/bulbous formations.
From Latin fungi (plural form of fungus ).

1765 as noun, 1818 as adjective, from Medieval Latin fungibilis , from Latin fungor ( “ to perform, discharge a duty ” ) +‎ -ible ( “ able to ” ) . Originally a legal term, [ 1 ] going back to Roman law : res fungibiles ( “ replaceable things ” ) .
FUNG-US contains FUNG (BULL pattern). Fungus exhibits spherical/spreading growth patterns.

First appears c. 1555, in a translation by Richard Eden . From Middle French fusion , from Latin fūsiōnem (the accusative of fūsiō ), from fusus , past participle of fundō ( “ I pour, I melt ” ) (see also found ). Doublet of foison .
GALACTIC - same TOR root as galaxy, representing toroidal galactic structure.
GALAXY (milky circle). GAL + TOR = rotational toroidal structure of stars.
GAL-GAL reduplication of spherical root. G↔B gives BAL-BAL. Means wheel/rolling, representing spherical motion and centrifugal force.
GAL-IL contains spherical root with G↔B giving BAL. R↔L interchangeable liquids. Represents circular region (Galilee) or rolling motion.
From Italian physicist Luigi Galvani +‎ -o- +‎ -meter .
GAMAL contains MAL (variant of M-L nasals from ATOM). GA-MAL suggests creative atomic force with nasal interchange.

From Latin īnstīgātor ( “ stimulator ” ) , from īnstīgāre ( present infinitive of īnstīgō ( “ to incite , set on , stimulate , rouse or urge ” ) , possibly from Proto-Indo-European *(s)teyg- ( “ to be sharp , to stab ; to puncture ; to goad ” ) ) + -or (from -ō ( suffix forming masculine agent nouns ) , from Proto-Indo-European *-h₃onh₂- ( suffix forming nouns denoting authority or burden ) ); cognate with French instigateur . By surface analysis , instigate +‎ -or .

From Middle English institucioun , from Old French institution , from Latin institūtiō , from instituō ( “ to set up ” ) , from in- ( “ in, on ” ) + statuō ( “ to set up, establish ” ) . Equivalent to institute +‎ -ion .

From Late Middle English intens , intense ( “ ardent, fervent; extreme, great, intense ” ) , [ 1 ] borrowed from Old French intense (modern French intense ), or directly from its etymon Latin intēnsus ( “ strained, stretched tight; intense; attentive; violent; ( rare ) eager, intent ” ) , the perfect passive participle of intendō ( “ to stretch out, strain ” ) , [ 2 ] from in- ( prefix meaning ‘in, inside, within’ ) + tendō ( “ to extend, stretch ” ) (ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *tend- ( “ to extend, stretch ” ) ).

From Middle English interpreter , interpretour , etc., from Old French interpreteur , interpreteeur , etc., from Late Latin interpretātōr , from classical Latin interpretātus ( “ explained , translated ” ) + -or ( “ -er : forming agent nouns ” ) , from interpretārī ( “ to explain , to translate ” ) , from interpres ( “ go-between , translator ” ) + -ārī ( “ to be ~ed ” ) , q.v. From Middle English mercurie , borrowed from Latin Mercurius . The chemical name comes from the planet Mercury. In medieval alchemy, the seven known metals—quicksilver, gold, silver, copper, iron, lead, and tin—were associated with the seven planets. Quicksilver was associated with the fastest planet, named after the Roman god Mercury, associated with speed and mobility. The astrological symbol for the planet became one of the alchemical symbols for the metal, and Mercury became an alternative name for the metal. Mercury is the only metal for which the alchemical planetary name survives, as it was decided it was preferable to quicksilver as a chemical name.

From Middle English planete , from Old French planete , from Latin planeta , planetes , from Ancient Greek πλανήτης ( planḗtēs , “ wanderer ” ) (itself an ellipsis of ἀστέρες πλανῆται ( astéres planêtai , “ wandering stars ” ) ), from Ancient Greek πλανάω ( planáō , “ wander about, stray ” ) , of unknown origin. Cognate with Latin pālor ( “ wander about, stray ” ) , Old Norse flana ( “ to rush about ” ) , and Norwegian flanta ( “ to wander about ” ) . More at flaunt . So called because they have apparent motion, unlike the "fixed" stars. Originally including also the moon and sun but not the Earth; modern scientific sense of "world that orbits a star" is from 1630s in English. The Greek word is an enlarged form of πλάνης ( plánēs , “ who wanders around, wanderer ” ) , also "wandering star, planet", in medicine "unstable temperature."
PLANETARY. PLAN (wander) + TOR = bodies wandering in rotational orbits.
From 'planet' + 'oid' (resembling). Planets are spherical bodies (BULL). The suffix -oid means 'form/shape' and the word describes a sphere-like object.

Borrowed from German Plankton , coined by German zoologist and marine biologist Victor Hensen . By surface analysis , Ancient Greek πλαγκτός ( planktós , “ drifter ” ) +‎ -on . Ultimately from Ancient Greek πλαγκτόν ( planktón , “ drifting ” ) , neuter nominative of πλαγκτός ( planktós ) , from πλάζομαι ( plázomai , “ to wander, drift ” ) , from πλάζω ( plázō , “ to cause to wander, drive astray ” ) . By surface analysis , plankt- +‎ -on .

From Middle English plante , from Old English plante ( “ young tree or shrub, herb newly planted ” ) , from Proto-West Germanic *plantu , from Latin planta ( “ sprout, shoot, cutting ” ) . From Late Latin plasma ( “ mold ” ) , from Ancient Greek πλάσμα ( plásma , “ something formed ” ) .

Borrowed from New Latin tensor ( “ that which stretches ” ) , equivalent to tense +‎ -or . Anatomical sense from 1704. Introduced in the 1840s by William Rowan Hamilton as an algebraic quantity unrelated to the modern notion of tensor. The contemporary mathematical meaning was introduced (as German Tensor ) by Woldemar Voigt (1898) [ 1 ] and adopted in English from 1915 (in the context of general relativity ), obscuring the earlier Hamiltonian sense. The mathematical object is so named because an early application of tensors was the study of materials stretching under tension . (See, for example, Cauchy stress tensor on Wikipedia. Wikipedia )
